question: 10. A particle moves under a central force inversely proportional to the \( \boldsymbol{k} \) th power of the distance. If the particle describes a circle (the central force proceeding from a point on the circumference of the circle \( ) \), find \( k \).
solution:
Solution. Choose polar coordinates with pole at the center of force and initial ray a diameter of the circular orbit of the particle. The equation of the orbit is then
\[
r=A \cos \theta
\]
where \( A \) is the diameter of the circle.
The equations of motion are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d^{2} r}{d t^{2}}-r\left(\frac{d \theta}{d t}\right)^{2}=-\frac{1}{m} f \\
r \frac{\partial^{2} \theta}{\partial t^{2}}+2 \frac{d r}{d t} \frac{d \theta}{d t}=0
\end{array}
\]
where \( m \) is the mass of the particle and \( f \) is the magnitude of the central force. Since the sign is taken as negative, a positive \( f \) means an attractive force.

After multiplication by \( r \), equation (3) can be integrated to give
\[
r^{2} \frac{d \theta}{d t}=h
\]
which asserts that the angular momentum of the particle is constant.
Differentiating (1) twice, using (4), we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d r}{d t} & =-A \sin \theta \frac{d \theta}{d t}=-\frac{A h \sin \theta}{r^{2}} \\
\frac{d^{2} r}{d t^{2}} & =-\frac{A h \cos \theta}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d t}+\frac{2 A h \sin \theta}{r^{3}} \cdot \frac{d r}{d t} \\
& =-\frac{h^{2}}{r^{3}}-\frac{2 A^{2} h^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{r^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Then substituting in (2), we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{f}{m} & =-\frac{h^{2}}{r^{3}}-\frac{2 A^{2} h^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{r^{5}}-r\left(\frac{h}{r^{2}}\right)^{2} \\
& =-\frac{2 h^{2}}{r^{5}}\left(r^{2}+A^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta\right)=-\frac{2 A^{2} h^{2}}{r^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( f=2 m A^{2} h^{2} r^{-5} \) and \( k=5 \).
Remark. See F. R. Moulton, Introduction to Celestial Mechanics, Macmillan, 1902, for a discussion of the general inverse power law of attraction.

descriptive_long.question: 10. A particle moves under a central force inversely proportional to the \( \boldsymbol{powerindex} \) th power of the distance. If the particle describes a circle (the central force proceeding from a point on the circumference of the circle \( \) \), find \( powerindex \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Choose polar coordinates with pole at the center of force and initial ray a diameter of the circular orbit of the particle. The equation of the orbit is then
\[
radius = diameter \cos azimuth
\]
where \( diameter \) is the diameter of the circle.
The equations of motion are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d^{2} radius}{d duration^{2}}-radius\left(\frac{d azimuth}{d duration}\right)^{2}=-\frac{1}{massconst} centralforce \\
radius \frac{\partial^{2} azimuth}{\partial duration^{2}}+2 \frac{d radius}{d duration} \frac{d azimuth}{d duration}=0
\end{array}
\]
where \( massconst \) is the mass of the particle and \( centralforce \) is the magnitude of the central force. Since the sign is taken as negative, a positive \( centralforce \) means an attractive force.

After multiplication by \( radius \), equation (3) can be integrated to give
\[
radius^{2} \frac{d azimuth}{d duration}=angmoment
\]
which asserts that the angular momentum of the particle is constant.
Differentiating (1) twice, using (4), we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d radius}{d duration} & =-diameter \sin azimuth \frac{d azimuth}{d duration}=-\frac{diameter angmoment \sin azimuth}{radius^{2}} \\
\frac{d^{2} radius}{d duration^{2}} & =-\frac{diameter angmoment \cos azimuth}{radius^{2}} \cdot \frac{d azimuth}{d duration}+\frac{2 diameter angmoment \sin azimuth}{radius^{3}} \cdot \frac{d radius}{d duration} \\
& =-\frac{angmoment^{2}}{radius^{3}}-\frac{2 diameter^{2} angmoment^{2} \sin ^{2} azimuth}{radius^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Then substituting in (2), we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{centralforce}{massconst} & =-\frac{angmoment^{2}}{radius^{3}}-\frac{2 diameter^{2} angmoment^{2} \sin ^{2} azimuth}{radius^{5}}-radius\left(\frac{angmoment}{radius^{2}}\right)^{2} \\
& =-\frac{2 angmoment^{2}}{radius^{5}}\left(radius^{2}+diameter^{2} \sin ^{2} azimuth\right)=-\frac{2 diameter^{2} angmoment^{2}}{radius^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( centralforce=2 massconst diameter^{2} angmoment^{2} radius^{-5} \) and \( powerindex=5 \).
Remark. See F. R. Moulton, Introduction to Celestial Mechanics, Macmillan, 1902, for a discussion of the general inverse power law of attraction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 10. A particle moves under a central force inversely proportional to the \( \boldsymbol{lanterns} \) th power of the distance. If the particle describes a circle (the central force proceeding from a point on the circumference of the circle \( ) \), find \( lanterns \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Choose polar coordinates with pole at the center of force and initial ray a diameter of the circular orbit of the particle. The equation of the orbit is then
\[
sandcastle=marshmallow \cos moonlight
\]
where \( marshmallow \) is the diameter of the circle.
The equations of motion are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d^{2} sandcastle}{d pineapple^{2}}-sandcastle\left(\frac{d moonlight}{d pineapple}\right)^{2}=-\frac{1}{waterfall} snowflake \\
sandcastle \frac{\partial^{2} moonlight}{\partial pineapple^{2}}+2 \frac{d sandcastle}{d pineapple} \frac{d moonlight}{d pineapple}=0
\end{array}
\]
where \( waterfall \) is the mass of the particle and \( snowflake \) is the magnitude of the central force. Since the sign is taken as negative, a positive \( snowflake \) means an attractive force.

After multiplication by \( sandcastle \), equation (3) can be integrated to give
\[
sandcastle^{2} \frac{d moonlight}{d pineapple}=blueberry
\]
which asserts that the angular momentum of the particle is constant.
Differentiating (1) twice, using (4), we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d sandcastle}{d pineapple} & =-marshmallow \sin moonlight \frac{d moonlight}{d pineapple}=-\frac{marshmallow blueberry \sin moonlight}{sandcastle^{2}} \\
\frac{d^{2} sandcastle}{d pineapple^{2}} & =-\frac{marshmallow blueberry \cos moonlight}{sandcastle^{2}} \cdot \frac{d moonlight}{d pineapple}+\frac{2 marshmallow^{2} blueberry \sin moonlight}{sandcastle^{3}} \cdot \frac{d sandcastle}{d pineapple} \\
& =-\frac{blueberry^{2}}{sandcastle^{3}}-\frac{2 marshmallow^{2} blueberry^{2} \sin ^{2} moonlight}{sandcastle^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Then substituting in (2), we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{snowflake}{waterfall} & =-\frac{blueberry^{2}}{sandcastle^{3}}-\frac{2 marshmallow^{2} blueberry^{2} \sin ^{2} moonlight}{sandcastle^{5}}-sandcastle\left(\frac{blueberry}{sandcastle^{2}}\right)^{2} \\
& =-\frac{2 blueberry^{2}}{sandcastle^{5}}\left(sandcastle^{2}+marshmallow^{2} \sin ^{2} moonlight\right)=-\frac{2 marshmallow^{2} blueberry^{2}}{sandcastle^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( snowflake=2 waterfall marshmallow^{2} blueberry^{2} sandcastle^{-5} \) and \( lanterns=5 \).
Remark. See F. R. Moulton, Introduction to Celestial Mechanics, Macmillan, 1902, for a discussion of the general inverse power law of attraction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 10. A particle moves under a central force inversely proportional to the \( \boldsymbol{rootvalue} \) th power of the distance. If the particle describes a circle (the central force proceeding from a point on the circumference of the circle \( ) \), find \( rootvalue \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Choose polar coordinates with pole at the center of force and initial ray a diameter of the circular orbit of the particle. The equation of the orbit is then
\[
angularcomp=singularity \cos straightlen
\]
where \( singularity \) is the diameter of the circle.
The equations of motion are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d^{2} angularcomp}{d spacemeasure^{2}}-angularcomp\left(\frac{d straightlen}{d spacemeasure}\right)^{2}=-\frac{1}{emptiness} placidity \\
angularcomp \frac{\partial^{2} straightlen}{\partial spacemeasure^{2}}+2 \frac{d angularcomp}{d spacemeasure} \frac{d straightlen}{d spacemeasure}=0
\end{array}
\]
where \( emptiness \) is the mass of the particle and \( placidity \) is the magnitude of the central force. Since the sign is taken as negative, a positive \( placidity \) means an attractive force.

After multiplication by \( angularcomp \), equation (3) can be integrated to give
\[
angularcomp^{2} \frac{d straightlen}{d spacemeasure}=idleness
\]
which asserts that the angular momentum of the particle is constant.
Differentiating (1) twice, using (4), we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d angularcomp}{d spacemeasure} & =-singularity \sin straightlen \frac{d straightlen}{d spacemeasure}=-\frac{singularity idleness \sin straightlen}{angularcomp^{2}} \\
\frac{d^{2} angularcomp}{d spacemeasure^{2}} & =-\frac{singularity idleness \cos straightlen}{angularcomp^{2}} \cdot \frac{d straightlen}{d spacemeasure}+\frac{2 singularity idleness \sin straightlen}{angularcomp^{3}} \cdot \frac{d angularcomp}{d spacemeasure} \\
& =-\frac{idleness^{2}}{angularcomp^{3}}-\frac{2 singularity^{2} idleness^{2} \sin ^{2} straightlen}{angularcomp^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Then substituting in (2), we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{placidity}{emptiness} & =-\frac{idleness^{2}}{angularcomp^{3}}-\frac{2 singularity^{2} idleness^{2} \sin ^{2} straightlen}{angularcomp^{5}}-angularcomp\left(\frac{idleness}{angularcomp^{2}}\right)^{2} \\
& =-\frac{2 idleness^{2}}{angularcomp^{5}}\left(angularcomp^{2}+singularity^{2} \sin ^{2} straightlen\right)=-\frac{2 singularity^{2} idleness^{2}}{angularcomp^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( placidity=2 emptiness singularity^{2} idleness^{2} angularcomp^{-5} \) and \( rootvalue=5 \).
Remark. See F. R. Moulton, Introduction to Celestial Mechanics, Macmillan, 1902, for a discussion of the general inverse power law of attraction.

garbled_string.question: 10. A particle moves under a central force inversely proportional to the \( \boldsymbol{zarlequi} \) th power of the distance. If the particle describes a circle (the central force proceeding from a point on the circumference of the circle \( ) \), find \( zarlequi \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Choose polar coordinates with pole at the center of force and initial ray a diameter of the circular orbit of the particle. The equation of the orbit is then
\[
quxmplyz=molfaxen \cos bnavziro
\]
where \( molfaxen \) is the diameter of the circle.
The equations of motion are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\frac{d^{2} quxmplyz}{d yzpkdvos^{2}}-quxmplyz\left(\frac{d bnavziro}{d yzpkdvos}\right)^{2}=-\frac{1}{tebidros} latimnuw \\
quxmplyz \frac{\partial^{2} bnavziro}{\partial yzpkdvos^{2}}+2 \frac{d quxmplyz}{d yzpkdvos} \frac{d bnavziro}{d yzpkdvos}=0
\end{array}
\]
where \( tebidros \) is the mass of the particle and \( latimnuw \) is the magnitude of the central force. Since the sign is taken as negative, a positive \( latimnuw \) means an attractive force.

After multiplication by \( quxmplyz \), equation (3) can be integrated to give
\[
quxmplyz^{2} \frac{d bnavziro}{d yzpkdvos}=qudictar
\]
which asserts that the angular momentum of the particle is constant.
Differentiating (1) twice, using (4), we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d quxmplyz}{d yzpkdvos} & =-molfaxen \sin bnavziro \frac{d bnavziro}{d yzpkdvos}=-\frac{molfaxen qudictar \sin bnavziro}{quxmplyz^{2}} \\
\frac{d^{2} quxmplyz}{d yzpkdvos^{2}} & =-\frac{molfaxen qudictar \cos bnavziro}{quxmplyz^{2}} \cdot \frac{d bnavziro}{d yzpkdvos}+\frac{2 molfaxen qudictar \sin bnavziro}{quxmplyz^{3}} \cdot \frac{d quxmplyz}{d yzpkdvos} \\
& =-\frac{qudictar^{2}}{quxmplyz^{3}}-\frac{2 molfaxen^{2} qudictar^{2} \sin ^{2} bnavziro}{quxmplyz^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Then substituting in (2), we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{latimnuw}{tebidros} & =-\frac{qudictar^{2}}{quxmplyz^{3}}-\frac{2 molfaxen^{2} qudictar^{2} \sin ^{2} bnavziro}{quxmplyz^{5}}-quxmplyz\left(\frac{qudictar}{quxmplyz^{2}}\right)^{2} \\
& =-\frac{2 qudictar^{2}}{quxmplyz^{5}}\left(quxmplyz^{2}+molfaxen^{2} \sin ^{2} bnavziro\right)=-\frac{2 molfaxen^{2} qudictar^{2}}{quxmplyz^{5}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus \( latimnuw=2 tebidros molfaxen^{2} qudictar^{2} quxmplyz^{-5} \) and \( zarlequi=5 \).
Remark. See F. R. Moulton, Introduction to Celestial Mechanics, Macmillan, 1902, for a discussion of the general inverse power law of attraction.

kernel_variant.question:
A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ moves in a fixed plane $\Pi$.  
At the point $O\in\Pi$ an attractive central force of magnitude  

\[
\lvert F_{c}(r)\rvert \;=\;K\,r^{-k},
\qquad K>0,\;k\in\mathbb{R},
\tag{1}
\]

acts on the particle, where $r$ denotes the distance to $O$.  
On $\Pi$ there is a uniform magnetic field  

\[
\mathbf{B}=B\,\mathbf{k},\qquad \mathbf{k}\ \hbox{parallel to the positive $z$-axis},
\]

so that only $F_{c}$ and the Lorentz force  

\[
\mathbf{F}_{L}=q\,\mathbf{v}\times\mathbf{B}
\]

act on the particle.  
The observed trajectory is the circle  

\[
\Gamma:=\bigl\{P\in\Pi:\;CP=\ell\bigr\},\qquad OC=\ell ,
\tag{2}
\]

i.e.\ $\Gamma$ has centre $C$ on the $x$-axis, radius $\ell$, and its circumference passes through $O$.  
Plane polar coordinates $(r,\theta)$ are taken with pole $O$ and initial ray $OC$; in these  

\[
r(\theta)=2\ell\cos\theta,
\qquad -\frac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}.
\tag{3}
\]

Throughout set  

\[
\mu:=\frac{qB}{2m}.
\]

Empirical facts  
(A) the geometrical areal velocity with respect to $O$, i.e.\ $\tfrac12 r^{2}\dot\theta$, is constant;  
(B) the geometric locus of the particle is exactly $\Gamma$.

Tasks  

1.  Prove that (A) and (B) cannot hold simultaneously when $B\neq0$; a perpendicular uniform magnetic field is incompatible with a constant areal velocity on the off-centred circle $\Gamma$.

2.  Put $B=0$.  Show that (1)-(3) admit a motion only when  

    \[
        k=5,
        \tag{4}
    \]

    and that the constant of areas $h:=r^{2}\dot\theta$ and the force
    constant $K$ are related by  

    \[
        K \;=\;2m\,h^{2}\,A^{2}
          \;=\;8m\,h^{2}\,\ell^{2},
        \qquad\bigl(A=2\ell\bigr).
        \tag{5}
    \]

3.  Let $\varphi(t)$ be the polar angle of the particle with respect
    to $C$.  Derive  

    \[
        \dot\varphi(t)=\omega_{0}\sec^{2}\theta(t),
        \qquad
        \omega_{0}:=\frac{h}{2\ell^{2}},
        \tag{6}
    \]

    and show that $\dot\varphi$ attains its minimum
    $\omega_{\min}=\omega_{0}$ at $\theta=0$
    (point $P=C+\ell$) and diverges as $\theta\to\pm\pi/2$.
    Check that $\omega_{\min}$ coincides with the value obtained by equating the centripetal acceleration $\ell\omega^{2}$ to the projection of the central force on $CP$.

4.  Impose at $t=0$ a small radial displacement
    $r(0)=r_{\Gamma}(0)+\varepsilon$, $0<\varepsilon\ll\ell$, leaving
    $\dot\theta(0)$ unchanged.  Let
    $r(t)=r_{\Gamma}(t)+\delta r(t)$ with $\delta r(0)=\varepsilon$,
    $\delta\dot r(0)=0$.  

    (a) Prove that, to first order in $\delta r$,  

    \[
       \boxed{%
       \delta\ddot r+\Xi(t)\,\delta r=0},
       \qquad
       \Xi(t):=\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}\cos^{-6}\theta(t)
               \Bigl(3\cos^{2}\theta(t)-10\Bigr).
       \tag{7}
    \]

    (b) Show that $\Xi(t)<0$ for all $t$ and that  

    \[
       -10\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}
       \;\le\;
       \Xi(t)
       \;\le\;
       -7\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}.
       \tag{8}
    \]

    (c) Conclude that the perturbation $\delta r(t)$ grows exponentially; the orbit $\Gamma$ is therefore \emph{linearly unstable}.

5.  Return to $B\neq0$ with no restriction on the orbit.
    Using the symmetric gauge
    ${\bf A}=\tfrac12{\bf B}\times{\bf r}$ show that the canonical
    $z$-component of the angular momentum is  

    \[
        L_{z}=m r^{2}\dot\theta+\frac{qB}{2}\,r^{2},
        \tag{9}
    \]

    and explain why the conservation of $L_{z}$ does not imply a
    constant geometric areal velocity when $B\neq0$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

\[
A:=2\ell,
\qquad
r(\theta)=A\cos\theta
\quad\bigl(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr).
\tag{10}
\]

The polar basis vectors satisfy  

\[
\mathbf{e}_{r}\times\mathbf{k}=-\mathbf{e}_{\theta},
\qquad
\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\times\mathbf{k}=+\mathbf{e}_{r}.
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
1.  Equations of motion and incompatibility of $B\neq0$  

The velocity is $\mathbf{v}=\dot r\,\mathbf{e}_{r}+r\dot\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{\theta}$.
Hence the Lorentz acceleration is  

\[
\mathbf{a}_{L}=\frac{q}{m}\,\mathbf{v}\times\mathbf{B}
           =2\mu\bigl(r\dot\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{r}-\dot r\,\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\bigr).
\]

Adding the central acceleration
$-(K/m)\,r^{-k}\mathbf{e}_{r}$ yields  

\[
\boxed{%
\begin{aligned}
\ddot r-r\dot\theta^{2}&=-\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-k}+2\mu r\dot\theta,\\[1mm]
r\ddot\theta+2\dot r\dot\theta&=-2\mu\dot r.
\end{aligned}}
\tag{11}
\]

From the $\theta$-equation  

\[
\frac{{\rm d}}{{\rm d}t}\bigl(r^{2}\dot\theta\bigr)
      =-2\mu r\dot r
      =-\mu\frac{{\rm d}}{{\rm d}t}(r^{2}),
\]

whence  

\[
\boxed{r^{2}\dot\theta+\mu r^{2}=h=\hbox{const}},
\quad
\dot\theta=\frac{h}{r^{2}}-\mu.
\tag{12}
\]

The areal velocity with respect to $O$ is  

\[
\frac12 r^{2}\dot\theta=\frac{h}{2}-\frac{\mu}{2}\,r^{2}.
\tag{13}
\]

Because $r(\theta)$ varies along $\Gamma$, constancy of
$\tfrac12 r^{2}\dot\theta$ forces $\mu=0$, i.e.\ $B=0$.  
Tasks (A) and (B) cannot both hold when $B\neq0$, completing Task 1.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
2.  Determination of $k$ and of the relation between $h$ and $K$  

Set $B=\mu=0$.  Equation (11) reduces to  

\[
\ddot r-r\dot\theta^{2}=-\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-k}.
\tag{14}
\]

Since $h=r^{2}\dot\theta$ is constant,

\[
\dot\theta=\frac{h}{r^{2}},
\qquad
\dot r=\frac{{\rm d}r}{{\rm d}\theta}\dot\theta
      =-\frac{h\sin\theta}{A\cos^{2}\theta},
\tag{15}
\]

and

\[
\ddot r=\frac{{\rm d}}{{\rm d}\theta}\bigl(\dot r\bigr)\dot\theta
       =-\frac{h^{2}}{A^{3}}
         \Bigl(2\cos^{-5}\theta-\cos^{-3}\theta\Bigr).
\tag{16}
\]

Moreover  

\[
r\dot\theta^{2}=A\cos\theta\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}\cos^{4}\theta}
               =\frac{h^{2}}{A^{3}}\cos^{-3}\theta.
\tag{17}
\]

Substituting (16)-(17) and $r=A\cos\theta$ into (14) gives  

\[
-\frac{2h^{2}}{A^{3}}\cos^{-5}\theta
     =-\frac{K}{m}\,A^{-k}\cos^{-k}\theta,
\]

which holds for all $\theta$ only if  

\[
k=5,
\qquad
K=2m\,h^{2}\,A^{2}
   =8m\,h^{2}\,\ell^{2}.
\]

This establishes (4)-(5) and completes Task 2.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
3.  Angular velocity with respect to $C$  

Cartesian coordinates give  

\[
x=r\cos\theta=A\cos^{2}\theta,
\qquad
y=r\sin\theta=A\cos\theta\sin\theta.
\]

Hence  

\[
\boldsymbol{\rho}:=\overrightarrow{CP}
=(x-\ell,\;y)
=(\ell\cos2\theta,\;\ell\sin2\theta),
\quad\lvert\boldsymbol{\rho}\rvert=\ell,
\]

so that $\varphi=2\theta$.  Therefore  

\[
\dot\varphi=2\dot\theta
           =\frac{2h}{r^{2}}
           =\frac{2h}{A^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}
           =\omega_{0}\sec^{2}\theta,
\qquad
\omega_{0}=\frac{h}{2\ell^{2}}.
\]

The minimum $\dot\varphi_{\min}=\omega_{0}$ is reached at $\theta=0$,
while $\dot\varphi\to\infty$ when $\theta\to\pm\pi/2$.  
At $\theta=0$: $r=A$, $\ell=A/2$, $v=h/A$ and the radial force gives  

\[
\ell\omega^{2}
=\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-5}
=\frac{2h^{2}A^{2}}{A^{5}}
=\frac{h^{2}}{4\ell^{3}},
\]
so $\omega=h/(2\ell^{2})=\omega_{0}$, confirming the result.  
Task 3 is finished.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
4.  Linear radial (in)stability of $\Gamma$  

Write $r(t)=r_{\Gamma}(t)+\delta r(t)$ with
$\lvert\delta r\rvert\ll\ell$, $\delta r(0)=\varepsilon$,
$\delta\dot r(0)=0$.  
Because $k=5$ the exact radial equation is  

\[
\ddot r-h^{2}r^{-3}=-\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-5},
\qquad
K=2m h^{2}A^{2}.
\tag{18}
\]

Expanding (18) to first order in $\delta r$ gives the variational
equation

\[
\delta\ddot r
+\Bigl(3h^{2}r_{\Gamma}^{-4}-5K\,m^{-1}r_{\Gamma}^{-6}\Bigr)\delta r
=0.
\tag{19}
\]

With $r_{\Gamma}=A\cos\theta(t)$ and (5) this becomes the Hill-type
equation

\[
\boxed{%
\delta\ddot r+\Xi(t)\,\delta r=0},
\qquad
\Xi(t)=\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}\cos^{-6}\theta(t)
       \bigl(3\cos^{2}\theta(t)-10\bigr),
\tag{20}
\]

establishing (7).  

Because $0<\cos^{2}\theta(t)\le1$ we have  

\[
-10\;\le\;3\cos^{2}\theta(t)-10\;\le\;-7,
\]

so  

\[
-10\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}
\;\le\;
\Xi(t)
\;\le\;
-7\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}
<0,
\tag{21}
\]

which is precisely (8).

Inequality (21) implies  
$\Xi(t)\le-\lambda^{2}$ with
$\lambda:=\sqrt{7}\,h/A^{2}>0$.  
Sturm comparison (or a standard Gronwall argument) now shows that any
non-trivial solution satisfies  

\[
\lvert\delta r(t)\rvert\;\ge\;
\lvert\varepsilon\rvert\cosh\bigl(\lambda t\bigr),
\]

so $\lvert\delta r(t)\rvert$ grows at least exponentially.  The orbit
$\Gamma$ is therefore linearly \emph{unstable}.  
This completes Task 4.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
5.  Canonical angular momentum for $B\neq0$  

With the symmetric gauge
${\bf A}=\tfrac12{\bf B}\times{\bf r}$ the Lagrangian is  

\[
L=\tfrac12 m\bigl(\dot r^{2}+r^{2}\dot\theta^{2}\bigr)
  +\frac{qB}{2}\,r^{2}\dot\theta
  -\frac{K}{4}\,r^{-4}.
\]

Because $\theta$ is cyclic,  

\[
\boxed{%
L_{z}=m r^{2}\dot\theta+\frac{qB}{2}\,r^{2}}\;=\text{const}.
\tag{22}
\]

The first summand is the mechanical angular momentum, the second the
field contribution.  Since the second term is proportional to $r^{2}(t)$, the
constancy of $L_{z}$ does \emph{not} imply that
$r^{2}\dot\theta$ (and hence the areal velocity) is constant whenever
$B\neq0$.  
Task 5 (and the whole problem) is finished. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Additional interacting forces:  the presence of the Lorentz force makes the
   angular-momentum integral non-standard and forces the use of canonical
   (rather than merely kinematic) conservation laws.

•  Higher technical level:  determining k now requires handling three different
   θ-dependent powers and proving their compatibility; a single cancellation
   does not work, so a systematic power comparison is unavoidable.

•  Deeper theory:  Step 4 uses linearisation of a non-autonomous Hill equation
   and the effective potential method to discuss stability—concepts that do not
   appear in the original exercise.

•  Multi-part structure:  the candidate must (i) establish the canonical integral,
   (ii) build and manipulate non-trivial θ-dependent expressions, (iii) perform
   a stability analysis, and (iv) interpret electromagnetically the conserved
   quantity—all of which go far beyond the purely geometric trigonometric
   manipulations of the original problem.

•  Independence checks:  the solution has to recover the classical k = 5 result
   when the magnetic field is switched off, and to link all pieces consistently
   through equation (17).  Any shortcut or pattern-matching attempt fails
   because each step hinges on cancelling *three* θ-dependent powers instead
   of one.

Hence the enhanced kernel variant is significantly more complex, demands a
broader mathematical toolkit (differential equations, canonical mechanics,
linear stability theory and electromagnetic dynamics) and requires more
substantial explicit computation than both the original problem and the
simple kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ moves in a fixed plane $\Pi$.  
At the point $O\in\Pi$ an attractive central force of magnitude  

\[
\lvert F_{c}(r)\rvert \;=\;K\,r^{-k},
\qquad K>0,\;k\in\mathbb{R},
\tag{1}
\]

acts on the particle, where $r$ denotes the distance to $O$.  
On $\Pi$ there is a uniform magnetic field  

\[
\mathbf{B}=B\,\mathbf{k},\qquad \mathbf{k}\ \hbox{parallel to the positive $z$-axis},
\]

so that only $F_{c}$ and the Lorentz force  

\[
\mathbf{F}_{L}=q\,\mathbf{v}\times\mathbf{B}
\]

act on the particle.  
The observed trajectory is the circle  

\[
\Gamma:=\bigl\{P\in\Pi:\;CP=\ell\bigr\},\qquad OC=\ell ,
\tag{2}
\]

i.e.\ $\Gamma$ has centre $C$ on the $x$-axis, radius $\ell$, and its circumference passes through $O$.  
Plane polar coordinates $(r,\theta)$ are taken with pole $O$ and initial ray $OC$; in these  

\[
r(\theta)=2\ell\cos\theta,
\qquad -\frac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}.
\tag{3}
\]

Throughout set  

\[
\mu:=\frac{qB}{2m}.
\]

Empirical facts  
(A) the geometrical areal velocity with respect to $O$, i.e.\ $\tfrac12 r^{2}\dot\theta$, is constant;  
(B) the geometric locus of the particle is exactly $\Gamma$.

Tasks  

1.  Prove that (A) and (B) cannot hold simultaneously when $B\neq0$; a perpendicular uniform magnetic field is incompatible with a constant areal velocity on the off-centred circle $\Gamma$.

2.  Put $B=0$.  Show that (1)-(3) admit a motion only when  

    \[
        k=5,
        \tag{4}
    \]

    and that the constant of areas $h:=r^{2}\dot\theta$ and the force
    constant $K$ are related by  

    \[
        K \;=\;2m\,h^{2}\,A^{2}
          \;=\;8m\,h^{2}\,\ell^{2},
        \qquad\bigl(A=2\ell\bigr).
        \tag{5}
    \]

3.  Let $\varphi(t)$ be the polar angle of the particle with respect
    to $C$.  Derive  

    \[
        \dot\varphi(t)=\omega_{0}\sec^{2}\theta(t),
        \qquad
        \omega_{0}:=\frac{h}{2\ell^{2}},
        \tag{6}
    \]

    and show that $\dot\varphi$ attains its minimum
    $\omega_{\min}=\omega_{0}$ at $\theta=0$
    (point $P=C+\ell$) and diverges as $\theta\to\pm\pi/2$.
    Check that $\omega_{\min}$ coincides with the value obtained by equating the centripetal acceleration $\ell\omega^{2}$ to the projection of the central force on $CP$.

4.  Impose at $t=0$ a small radial displacement
    $r(0)=r_{\Gamma}(0)+\varepsilon$, $0<\varepsilon\ll\ell$, leaving
    $\dot\theta(0)$ unchanged.  Let
    $r(t)=r_{\Gamma}(t)+\delta r(t)$ with $\delta r(0)=\varepsilon$,
    $\delta\dot r(0)=0$.  

    (a) Prove that, to first order in $\delta r$,  

    \[
       \boxed{%
       \delta\ddot r+\Xi(t)\,\delta r=0},
       \qquad
       \Xi(t):=\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}\cos^{-6}\theta(t)
               \Bigl(3\cos^{2}\theta(t)-10\Bigr).
       \tag{7}
    \]

    (b) Show that $\Xi(t)<0$ for all $t$ and that  

    \[
       -10\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}
       \;\le\;
       \Xi(t)
       \;\le\;
       -7\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}.
       \tag{8}
    \]

    (c) Conclude that the perturbation $\delta r(t)$ grows exponentially; the orbit $\Gamma$ is therefore \emph{linearly unstable}.

5.  Return to $B\neq0$ with no restriction on the orbit.
    Using the symmetric gauge
    ${\bf A}=\tfrac12{\bf B}\times{\bf r}$ show that the canonical
    $z$-component of the angular momentum is  

    \[
        L_{z}=m r^{2}\dot\theta+\frac{qB}{2}\,r^{2},
        \tag{9}
    \]

    and explain why the conservation of $L_{z}$ does not imply a
    constant geometric areal velocity when $B\neq0$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

\[
A:=2\ell,
\qquad
r(\theta)=A\cos\theta
\quad\bigl(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr).
\tag{10}
\]

The polar basis vectors satisfy  

\[
\mathbf{e}_{r}\times\mathbf{k}=-\mathbf{e}_{\theta},
\qquad
\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\times\mathbf{k}=+\mathbf{e}_{r}.
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
1.  Equations of motion and incompatibility of $B\neq0$  

The velocity is $\mathbf{v}=\dot r\,\mathbf{e}_{r}+r\dot\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{\theta}$.
Hence the Lorentz acceleration is  

\[
\mathbf{a}_{L}=\frac{q}{m}\,\mathbf{v}\times\mathbf{B}
           =2\mu\bigl(r\dot\theta\,\mathbf{e}_{r}-\dot r\,\mathbf{e}_{\theta}\bigr).
\]

Adding the central acceleration
$-(K/m)\,r^{-k}\mathbf{e}_{r}$ yields  

\[
\boxed{%
\begin{aligned}
\ddot r-r\dot\theta^{2}&=-\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-k}+2\mu r\dot\theta,\\[1mm]
r\ddot\theta+2\dot r\dot\theta&=-2\mu\dot r.
\end{aligned}}
\tag{11}
\]

From the $\theta$-equation  

\[
\frac{{\rm d}}{{\rm d}t}\bigl(r^{2}\dot\theta\bigr)
      =-2\mu r\dot r
      =-\mu\frac{{\rm d}}{{\rm d}t}(r^{2}),
\]

whence  

\[
\boxed{r^{2}\dot\theta+\mu r^{2}=h=\hbox{const}},
\quad
\dot\theta=\frac{h}{r^{2}}-\mu.
\tag{12}
\]

The areal velocity with respect to $O$ is  

\[
\frac12 r^{2}\dot\theta=\frac{h}{2}-\frac{\mu}{2}\,r^{2}.
\tag{13}
\]

Because $r(\theta)$ varies along $\Gamma$, constancy of
$\tfrac12 r^{2}\dot\theta$ forces $\mu=0$, i.e.\ $B=0$.  
Tasks (A) and (B) cannot both hold when $B\neq0$, completing Task 1.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
2.  Determination of $k$ and of the relation between $h$ and $K$  

Set $B=\mu=0$.  Equation (11) reduces to  

\[
\ddot r-r\dot\theta^{2}=-\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-k}.
\tag{14}
\]

Since $h=r^{2}\dot\theta$ is constant,

\[
\dot\theta=\frac{h}{r^{2}},
\qquad
\dot r=\frac{{\rm d}r}{{\rm d}\theta}\dot\theta
      =-\frac{h\sin\theta}{A\cos^{2}\theta},
\tag{15}
\]

and

\[
\ddot r=\frac{{\rm d}}{{\rm d}\theta}\bigl(\dot r\bigr)\dot\theta
       =-\frac{h^{2}}{A^{3}}
         \Bigl(2\cos^{-5}\theta-\cos^{-3}\theta\Bigr).
\tag{16}
\]

Moreover  

\[
r\dot\theta^{2}=A\cos\theta\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}\cos^{4}\theta}
               =\frac{h^{2}}{A^{3}}\cos^{-3}\theta.
\tag{17}
\]

Substituting (16)-(17) and $r=A\cos\theta$ into (14) gives  

\[
-\frac{2h^{2}}{A^{3}}\cos^{-5}\theta
     =-\frac{K}{m}\,A^{-k}\cos^{-k}\theta,
\]

which holds for all $\theta$ only if  

\[
k=5,
\qquad
K=2m\,h^{2}\,A^{2}
   =8m\,h^{2}\,\ell^{2}.
\]

This establishes (4)-(5) and completes Task 2.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
3.  Angular velocity with respect to $C$  

Cartesian coordinates give  

\[
x=r\cos\theta=A\cos^{2}\theta,
\qquad
y=r\sin\theta=A\cos\theta\sin\theta.
\]

Hence  

\[
\boldsymbol{\rho}:=\overrightarrow{CP}
=(x-\ell,\;y)
=(\ell\cos2\theta,\;\ell\sin2\theta),
\quad\lvert\boldsymbol{\rho}\rvert=\ell,
\]

so that $\varphi=2\theta$.  Therefore  

\[
\dot\varphi=2\dot\theta
           =\frac{2h}{r^{2}}
           =\frac{2h}{A^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}
           =\omega_{0}\sec^{2}\theta,
\qquad
\omega_{0}=\frac{h}{2\ell^{2}}.
\]

The minimum $\dot\varphi_{\min}=\omega_{0}$ is reached at $\theta=0$,
while $\dot\varphi\to\infty$ when $\theta\to\pm\pi/2$.  
At $\theta=0$: $r=A$, $\ell=A/2$, $v=h/A$ and the radial force gives  

\[
\ell\omega^{2}
=\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-5}
=\frac{2h^{2}A^{2}}{A^{5}}
=\frac{h^{2}}{4\ell^{3}},
\]
so $\omega=h/(2\ell^{2})=\omega_{0}$, confirming the result.  
Task 3 is finished.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
4.  Linear radial (in)stability of $\Gamma$  

Write $r(t)=r_{\Gamma}(t)+\delta r(t)$ with
$\lvert\delta r\rvert\ll\ell$, $\delta r(0)=\varepsilon$,
$\delta\dot r(0)=0$.  
Because $k=5$ the exact radial equation is  

\[
\ddot r-h^{2}r^{-3}=-\frac{K}{m}\,r^{-5},
\qquad
K=2m h^{2}A^{2}.
\tag{18}
\]

Expanding (18) to first order in $\delta r$ gives the variational
equation

\[
\delta\ddot r
+\Bigl(3h^{2}r_{\Gamma}^{-4}-5K\,m^{-1}r_{\Gamma}^{-6}\Bigr)\delta r
=0.
\tag{19}
\]

With $r_{\Gamma}=A\cos\theta(t)$ and (5) this becomes the Hill-type
equation

\[
\boxed{%
\delta\ddot r+\Xi(t)\,\delta r=0},
\qquad
\Xi(t)=\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}\cos^{-6}\theta(t)
       \bigl(3\cos^{2}\theta(t)-10\bigr),
\tag{20}
\]

establishing (7).  

Because $0<\cos^{2}\theta(t)\le1$ we have  

\[
-10\;\le\;3\cos^{2}\theta(t)-10\;\le\;-7,
\]

so  

\[
-10\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}
\;\le\;
\Xi(t)
\;\le\;
-7\,\frac{h^{2}}{A^{4}}
<0,
\tag{21}
\]

which is precisely (8).

Inequality (21) implies  
$\Xi(t)\le-\lambda^{2}$ with
$\lambda:=\sqrt{7}\,h/A^{2}>0$.  
Sturm comparison (or a standard Gronwall argument) now shows that any
non-trivial solution satisfies  

\[
\lvert\delta r(t)\rvert\;\ge\;
\lvert\varepsilon\rvert\cosh\bigl(\lambda t\bigr),
\]

so $\lvert\delta r(t)\rvert$ grows at least exponentially.  The orbit
$\Gamma$ is therefore linearly \emph{unstable}.  
This completes Task 4.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
5.  Canonical angular momentum for $B\neq0$  

With the symmetric gauge
${\bf A}=\tfrac12{\bf B}\times{\bf r}$ the Lagrangian is  

\[
L=\tfrac12 m\bigl(\dot r^{2}+r^{2}\dot\theta^{2}\bigr)
  +\frac{qB}{2}\,r^{2}\dot\theta
  -\frac{K}{4}\,r^{-4}.
\]

Because $\theta$ is cyclic,  

\[
\boxed{%
L_{z}=m r^{2}\dot\theta+\frac{qB}{2}\,r^{2}}\;=\text{const}.
\tag{22}
\]

The first summand is the mechanical angular momentum, the second the
field contribution.  Since the second term is proportional to $r^{2}(t)$, the
constancy of $L_{z}$ does \emph{not} imply that
$r^{2}\dot\theta$ (and hence the areal velocity) is constant whenever
$B\neq0$.  
Task 5 (and the whole problem) is finished. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Additional interacting forces:  the presence of the Lorentz force makes the
   angular-momentum integral non-standard and forces the use of canonical
   (rather than merely kinematic) conservation laws.

•  Higher technical level:  determining k now requires handling three different
   θ-dependent powers and proving their compatibility; a single cancellation
   does not work, so a systematic power comparison is unavoidable.

•  Deeper theory:  Step 4 uses linearisation of a non-autonomous Hill equation
   and the effective potential method to discuss stability—concepts that do not
   appear in the original exercise.

•  Multi-part structure:  the candidate must (i) establish the canonical integral,
   (ii) build and manipulate non-trivial θ-dependent expressions, (iii) perform
   a stability analysis, and (iv) interpret electromagnetically the conserved
   quantity—all of which go far beyond the purely geometric trigonometric
   manipulations of the original problem.

•  Independence checks:  the solution has to recover the classical k = 5 result
   when the magnetic field is switched off, and to link all pieces consistently
   through equation (17).  Any shortcut or pattern-matching attempt fails
   because each step hinges on cancelling *three* θ-dependent powers instead
   of one.

Hence the enhanced kernel variant is significantly more complex, demands a
broader mathematical toolkit (differential equations, canonical mechanics,
linear stability theory and electromagnetic dynamics) and requires more
substantial explicit computation than both the original problem and the
simple kernel variant.
